senses.gloss: to void dung, to evacuate, to throw out ordure (said of a cloven-hoofed beast)

examples.text: نَهَانَا رَسُولِ اللَّهِ صَلَّى اللّٰهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ أَنْ نَتَمَسَّحَ بِعَظْمٍ أَوْ بَعْرٍ.
examples.english: The Prophet (PBUH) forbade us to wipe ourselves with bone or dung.

examples.text: وَقَدْ كَانَتْ إِحْدَاكُنَّ فِي الْجَاهِلِيَّةِ تَرْمِي بِالْبَعَرَةِ عَلَى رَأْسِ الْحَوْلِ.
examples.english: In the pre-Islamic time one of your kind (a widow) used to cast a globe of dung when a year has elapsed.

etymology.text: From بَعِير (baʕīr, “camel”), or compare جَعْر (jaʕr, “dung”), جَعَرَ (jaʕara, “to void one’s dung”). The only parallel form in Semitic is Classical Syriac ܒܥܘܿܪܴܐ (bəʿōrā, “dried dung”), while the animal term is widely attested in Semitic, so it is unlikely that the animal name came from the name of dung – as Hommel suggested – which is also appealing semantically. Compare also Hebrew בָּעַר (“burn (intransitive)”), ביער (“dispose or destroy by burning”).
